title: Able Assess vs Nymbl
description: Side-by-side comparison of Able Assess and Nymbl. Objective clinical screening and risk stratification vs consumer balance exercise program.

seo.title: Able Assess vs Nymbl | Able Care
seo.description: Compare Able Assess with Nymbl. Clinical-grade falls risk screening with objective data vs balance training exercises. Screening vs wellness program.

blocks.headline: Able Assess vs Nymbl.
blocks.subtitle: Nymbl delivers balance exercises. Able Assess delivers objective clinical screening. One is a wellness program, the other is a clinical tool.

blocks.heading: Head-to-head comparison
blocks.tldr: Nymbl is a consumer-facing balance training app. It guides users through short daily exercises designed to improve balance over time. Able Assess is a clinical screening tool. It captures four objective, sensor-based functional health metrics, scores them against normative data, stratifies risk, and routes the result to the care team. Nymbl is an intervention. Able Assess is the screening that tells you who needs an intervention and whether that intervention is working.

values: Clinical falls risk screening (objective measurement and risk stratification)
Screen, stratify and track falls risk with objective data
Improve balance through daily exercise routines
Four objective metrics scored against age- and sex-matched normative data, with longitudinal trending
Exercise adherence and self-reported balance confidence
Peer-reviewed metrics, FDA listed, CE marked, aligned to NICE 2025 guidelines
Consumer wellness positioning, not a clinical screening tool
Any staff member runs a five-minute screening, no clinical certification required
Self-directed by the resident or client, minimal staff involvement
Risk banding dashboard with population-level reporting for operators and payers
Automated risk banding routed to the care team, EHR and OASIS integration-ready
No direct care plan integration, sits alongside the care workflow
Objective data trail for HHVBP, VBP and regulatory reporting
Wellness program, not typically accepted as clinical evidence by payers

blocks.heading: Why screening comes before exercise
blocks.pulledStat: Multifactorial falls risk screening outperforms single-test approaches in predicting falls in older adults.

citations.title: Effectiveness of multifactorial interventions in preventing falls among older adults in the community
Prognostic value of grip strength: findings from the Prospective Urban Rural Epidemiology (PURE) study
Falls in older people: assessing risk and prevention

blocks.heading: Screen first, then intervene.
blocks.bodyText: A 20-minute demo showing how objective screening identifies who needs intervention and tracks whether it is working.
